categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What does the date of July 14 correspond to?
options: Armistice of the First World War
explication: July 14 is the national holiday. It celebrates the storming of the Bastille (1789) and the Fête de la Fédération (1790), symbols of the end of the absolute monarchy.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is one of the symbols of the French Republic?
options: The blue, white, red tricolor flag
explication: The tricolor flag, Marianne, the motto, the national anthem and the seal are the main official symbols of the French Republic.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: The principle of equality means that:
options: The law is the same for everyone
Everyone must have the same opinions
There are no more rich or poor people
explication: Equality before the law guarantees that every citizen has the same rights and the same duties, without distinction of origin, sex or religion.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: "Liberty, equality, fraternity" is:
options: The first article of the Constitution
The title of the national anthem
The name of a political party
explication: This is the official motto of the French Republic. It can be found written on town halls, public schools and coins.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Do we have the right to publicly insult someone because they are different (disability, physical appearance, sex...)?
options: Yes, in the name of freedom of expression
No, it is an offense punishable by law
Yes, if the person is not French
explication: Discrimination and public insult based on difference (racism, sexism, homophobia, fatphobia, disability) are offenses severely punished by law.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Can certain jobs be reserved for men?
options: Yes, in the army and the police
No, the law guarantees professional equality
explication: The law guarantees strict equality between women and men in the working world. No job can be forbidden to a woman.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: When does the Constitution of the Fifth Republic date from?
explication: Our current Constitution was adopted in 1958, under the impetus of General de Gaulle. It founded the Fifth Republic.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: The regime of France is:
explication: France is an indivisible, secular, democratic and social Republic. Power belongs to the people who exercise it by voting.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Which of these symbols officially represents the French Republic?
explication: Marianne is the allegorical figure of the Republic. She represents freedom and reason. Her bust is present in all town halls.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Where can we see the motto of the Republic?
options: On the pediment of town halls
explication: The motto 'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity' is inscribed on public buildings of the State, such as town halls, prefectures and schools.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What does freedom mean?
options: The right to do whatever one wants without any limits
The power to do anything that does not harm others, in compliance with the law
explication: According to the Declaration of Human Rights, freedom consists in being able to do anything that does not harm others. It is framed by law to protect everyone.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What does the word "fraternity" mean in the French motto?
options: The legal obligation to have brothers and sisters
The fact that only men have power
explication: Fraternity represents solidarity and mutual aid between all members of society (for example, through Social Security or taxes).[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Which animal is a symbol of France?
explication: The Gallic rooster is a traditional symbol of France, often used during sports competitions or on war memorials.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the name of the national anthem?
explication: La Marseillaise, written by Rouget de Lisle in 1792, is the national anthem of France.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the official language of the French Republic?
explication: Article 2 of the Constitution specifies that "The language of the Republic is French". It is the only official language for administration and justice.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is traditionally organized on the Champs Élysées on July 14 to celebrate the national holiday?
explication: Every July 14, a large military parade is organized on the Avenue des Champs-Élysées in Paris, in the presence of the President of the Republic.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: In what year was the law separating Churches and State passed?
explication: The 1905 law is the founding text of modern secularism in France. It separates political power from religious power.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What does the principle of secularism allow?
options: The freedom to believe or not to believe
The obligation to choose an official religion
explication: Secularism guarantees freedom of conscience: everyone is free to practice a religion, to change religion or to have none.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Can a child refuse to go to school for religious reasons?
options: Yes, if it is authorized by his religion
No, instruction is compulsory and secular
explication: School is compulsory. Religion can in no case be an excuse to refuse to be educated or to refuse to take certain courses (like sports or sciences).[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Can a person freely change religion?
options: Yes, it is a right guaranteed by secularism
Only with the agreement of the town hall
Only if she is of French nationality
explication: Freedom of conscience includes the absolute right to choose one's religion, to change it whenever one wishes, or to stop believing.[cite: 1]

question: Who appoints the Prime Minister?
explication: The President of the Republic appoints the Prime Minister, who then becomes the head of government.[cite: 1]

question: The Parliament is composed of:
options: The National Assembly and the Senate
explication: The Parliament's role is to vote laws. It is composed of two assemblies: the National Assembly (the deputies) and the Senate (the senators).[cite: 1]

question: What is executive power?
options: The power to enforce laws and lead the country
The power to amend the Constitution
explication: Executive power is held by the President of the Republic and the government. Its role is to enforce the laws voted by Parliament and to manage the State.[cite: 1]

question: Leaders are elected by citizens in:
explication: In a democracy, power belongs to the people, who freely choose their representatives through elections.[cite: 1]

question: Do we have the right not to respect a law?
options: Yes, if we don't agree with it
No, ignorance of the law is no excuse to violate it
Yes, if the mayor authorizes it
explication: In France, the law applies to everyone equally. Non-compliance with the law leads to sanctions (fines, prison sentences).[cite: 1]

question: Who must respect the law?
explication: In a rule of law, every person present on the territory (citizens, foreigners, leaders, police officers) must obligatorily respect the law.[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of the judicial authority?
options: Enforce laws and sanction those who do not respect them
explication: The judicial authority (judges and courts) ensures compliance with laws, protects individual freedoms, settles conflicts and punishes offenses.[cite: 1]

question: What happens if a minister does not respect the law?
options: He cannot be punished because he is protected
He can be prosecuted and judged like any citizen
The president cancels the law for him
He just has to apologize publicly
explication: No one is above the law. A minister or elected official who commits an offense can be prosecuted and judged by the justice system.[cite: 1]

question: Who is elected during the legislative elections?
explication: Legislative elections are used to elect the deputies who sit in the National Assembly to vote laws.[cite: 1]

question: How many deputies make up the National Assembly?
explication: The National Assembly is composed of 577 deputies, elected by direct universal suffrage by French citizens.[cite: 1]

question: Who is elected during the municipal elections?
explication: Municipal elections allow the election of the municipal council. It is then these councilors who elect the mayor of the commune.[cite: 1]

question: From what age do you have the right to vote in France?
explication: The civil majority and the right to vote (for citizens of French nationality) are set at 18 years old.[cite: 1]

question: For how long is the President of the French Republic elected?
explication: The President of the Republic is elected for a 5-year term, which is called the quinquennat.[cite: 1]

question: Who holds the legislative power?
explication: Legislative power, which consists of proposing, debating and voting laws, belongs to Parliament.[cite: 1]

question: Who sanctions the author of a theft?
options: A judge of the judicial authority
explication: Only the justice system has the power to criminally sanction a person. It is forbidden to take justice into one's own hands.[cite: 1]

question: Who runs the commune?
explication: The mayor, assisted by his municipal council, is the head of the administration of the commune. He manages the local affairs of the city.[cite: 1]

question: Does the President of the Republic have all the powers?
options: No, powers are separated in France
Yes, because he appoints the judges
explication: France applies the principle of separation of powers (executive, legislative, judicial) in order to avoid dictatorship and guarantee democracy.[cite: 1]

question: Who is the prefect?
options: The representative of the State in the department
The head of the municipal police
A judge of the Court of Cassation
explication: Appointed by the president, the prefect represents the government in a department. He ensures the maintenance of public order and the application of laws.[cite: 1]

question: How many States are part of the European Union as of January 1, 2025?
explication: The European Union currently has 27 Member States, following the departure of the United Kingdom (Brexit).[cite: 1]

question: What is the currency used in France?
explication: The Euro is the official currency of France and a large majority of the member countries of the European Union since 2002.[cite: 1]

question: What is the name of the text that sets out the rights and duties of people residing in France?
options: The Constitution and the laws of the Republic
explication: The Constitution, along with the Declaration of Human Rights and the laws voted by Parliament, defines the rights, freedoms and duties of everyone.[cite: 1]

question: What year does the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen date from?
explication: Adopted at the beginning of the French Revolution in 1789, it lays the universal foundations of fundamental rights (freedom, equality, property).[cite: 1]

question: What right allows a person to defend themselves before justice?
options: The right to a fair trial (right to a lawyer)
explication: Anyone accused has the right to defend themselves, to be heard by an impartial judge and to be assisted by a lawyer during a fair trial.[cite: 1]

question: A woman can have an abortion:
options: Freely, within the time limits provided by law (IVG)
Only if she is over 18 years old
explication: Voluntary Interruption of Pregnancy (IVG) is a right in France, recently inscribed in the Constitution. The decision belongs to the woman alone.[cite: 1]

question: The death penalty is:
options: Used only for very serious crimes
explication: The death penalty was definitively abolished in France in 1981.[cite: 1]

question: In France, is it legal to be married to several people at the same time?
options: No, polygamy is forbidden and punished by law
Yes, if you have the financial means
explication: Polygamy is formally forbidden on French territory. Only monogamy is legal.[cite: 1]

question: What must a victim of violence do?
options: Report the facts to the police (17) or call an emergency number
explication: The law protects victims. You must contact the police (17) or call national help numbers (such as 3919 for domestic violence).[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of the police and the gendarmerie?
options: Ensure security, maintain order and search for the perpetrators of offenses
explication: Law enforcement protects the population and ensures compliance with the law. It is then the judges who decide on the sanctions.[cite: 1]

question: Throwing a bottle in the street is:
options: Authorized if there is no trash can
An offense punishable by a fine
An accepted practice in the evening
Legal if you are a pedestrian
explication: Respecting the environment and the cleanliness of the public space is a civic duty. Throwing garbage in the street is an offense.[cite: 1]

question: What are the risks for a person who does not respect the law?
options: Sanctions provided by justice (fine, prison, etc.)
explication: Non-compliance with the laws entails penalties proportional to the seriousness of the act, set by the judges (fines, community service, prison).[cite: 1]

question: In what year did the French Revolution begin?
explication: The French Revolution began in 1789, marked notably by the storming of the Bastille on July 14.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Napoleon I?
options: The first president of the Republic
explication: A brilliant military general, Napoleon Bonaparte became the Emperor of the French under the name of Napoleon I from 1804 to 1814.[cite: 1]

question: Which of these historical figures is French?
explication: Joan of Arc is a great figure in the history of France, famous for having helped repel the English during the Hundred Years' War in the 15th century.[cite: 1]

question: In which Republic are we today?
explication: We are under the Fifth Republic, established in 1958 by General de Gaulle.[cite: 1]

question: What is the Shoah?
options: A battle of the First World War
The name of a peace treaty
The systematic extermination of Jews by Nazi Germany
explication: The Shoah designates the genocide of approximately six million European Jews organized by the Nazi regime during the Second World War.[cite: 1]

question: Which country or region of the world was colonized by France?
explication: France possessed a vast colonial empire, including many countries in Africa (including Algeria, Senegal), Asia (Indochina) and America.[cite: 1]

question: Who made school free, secular and compulsory?
explication: It is thanks to the laws carried by the minister Jules Ferry in 1881 and 1882 that public primary school became free, compulsory and secular.[cite: 1]

question: When did the Second World War take place?
explication: The Second World War, the deadliest global conflict in history, took place from 1939 to 1945.[cite: 1]

question: When did the First World War take place?
explication: The First World War began in 1914 and ended with the Armistice of November 11, 1918.[cite: 1]

question: In what year was the European Economic Community (EEC) created?
explication: The EEC, the direct ancestor of the current European Union, was created by the Treaty of Rome in 1957, with France as a co-founding country.[cite: 1]

question: November 11 is a public holiday. What does this date correspond to?
options: Armistice of the First World War (1918)
End of the Second World War (1945)
explication: November 11 commemorates the Armistice signed in 1918, which ended the fighting of the First World War.[cite: 1]

question: Who was the first President of the Vth Republic?
explication: General de Gaulle, after having had the new Constitution adopted, was elected first president of the Vth Republic in 1958.[cite: 1]

question: In what year was slavery definitively abolished in France?
explication: Under the impetus of Victor Schœlcher, slavery was definitively abolished in the French colonies in 1848.[cite: 1]

question: Since what year has public school been free?
explication: The absolute free nature of primary education in public schools was established by the law of June 16, 1881 (Jules Ferry law).[cite: 1]

question: How many republics have there been in France?
explication: France has experienced five republics since the French Revolution. We currently live under the Vth Republic.[cite: 1]

question: Who was the king of France at the time of the French Revolution?
explication: Louis XVI was the king in place during the outbreak of the Revolution of 1789. He will eventually be judged and guillotined.[cite: 1]

question: Why is the year 1958 important for France?
options: It's the end of the monarchy
It's the year of the abolition of the death penalty
It's the creation of the Vth Republic
It's the victory of the World Cup
explication: The year 1958 marks the adoption of a new Constitution and the transition to the Fifth Republic, offering more power to the President.[cite: 1]

question: Which river flows in France?
explication: The Loire is the longest river in France. The Seine, the Rhône and the Garonne are the other major French rivers.[cite: 1]

question: Which city is French?
explication: Lyon, located in the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region, is one of the largest French metropolises.[cite: 1]

question: Which ocean borders the French west coast?
explication: The west coast of metropolitan France is bordered by the Atlantic Ocean (from Brittany to the Basque Country).[cite: 1]

question: What is the capital of France?
explication: Paris is the political, economic and cultural capital of France.[cite: 1]

question: On which continent is metropolitan France located?
explication: Metropolitan France, often called "the Hexagon", is located in Western Europe.[cite: 1]

question: Which island is a French overseas department?
explication: Guadeloupe, just like Martinique, Reunion and Mayotte, is an overseas department. (Corsica is in mainland France).[cite: 1]

question: How many regions are there in metropolitan France?
explication: Since the major reform of 2016, metropolitan France has been divided into 13 large administrative regions.[cite: 1]

question: Which city is a major seaport?
explication: Marseille, located on the Mediterranean coast, is the largest commercial port in France.[cite: 1]

question: What is the sea to the south of metropolitan France?
explication: The southern coast of France is bordered by the Mediterranean Sea, which separates Europe from Africa.[cite: 1]

question: Where is Corsica located?
explication: Corsica is a French island located in the Mediterranean Sea, south-east of the French mainland.[cite: 1]

question: Which mountain range is located between France and Italy?
explication: The Alps form a natural border between France and Italy. We find Mont Blanc there, the highest peak in Europe.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Molière?
options: A famous man of theater and actor
explication: Molière is the most famous French playwright of the 17th century. The French language is moreover often called "the language of Molière".[cite: 1]

question: Who was Charles Baudelaire?
explication: Charles Baudelaire is one of the greatest French poets of the 19th century, world-famous for his collection "Les Fleurs du mal".[cite: 1]

question: Who was George Sand?
options: A famous French novelist and writer of the 19th century
explication: Amantine Aurore Lucile Dupin, known under the male pseudonym of George Sand, is one of the greatest writers of French literature.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
options: A feminist philosopher and writer of the 20th century
explication: Simone de Beauvoir is a major figure in literature and feminism in France, famous in particular for her essay 'The Second Sex'.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Albert Camus?
options: A French writer and philosopher, Nobel Prize in Literature
explication: Albert Camus is a major author of the 20th century (author of 'The Stranger') who received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Paul Cézanne?
options: A famous French impressionist and post-impressionist painter
explication: Paul Cézanne is a major painter whose work linked 19th-century impressionism with 20th-century cubism.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Josephine Baker?
options: A French-American dancer, singer and resistance fighter
A nurse of the First World War
explication: A world-renowned music hall artist, she engaged in the Resistance during the war and was entered into the Pantheon in 2021.[cite: 1]

question: Which of these names is that of a famous French singer?
explication: Édith Piaf is one of the most famous French singers in the world, famous for titles like 'La Vie en rose' or 'L'Hymne à l'amour'.[cite: 1]

question: What is the Louvre?
options: One of the largest art and antiquities museums in the world
explication: Former royal palace located in Paris, the Louvre is today a world-famous museum which notably houses the Mona Lisa.[cite: 1]

question: Who was Jean de La Fontaine?
options: A 17th-century writer and poet, famous for his Fables
explication: Jean de La Fontaine wrote Fables featuring animals (like 'The Crow and the Fox') to teach moral lessons.[cite: 1]

question: In which city is the Eiffel Tower located?
explication: The Eiffel Tower, built by Gustave Eiffel for the Universal Exhibition of 1889, is the symbol monument of Paris and France.[cite: 1]

question: When do we celebrate Christmas?
explication: Christmas is celebrated on December 25. Although having a Christian origin, it is today a festival very widely celebrated throughout society.[cite: 1]

question: What emergency number makes it possible to call the SAMU (medical emergencies)?
explication: 15 is the SAMU (Service d'Aide Médicale Urgente) number for health emergencies (faintness, serious injury).[cite: 1]

question: What emergency number makes it possible to call the fire brigade?
explication: 18 is the firefighters' number, to call in case of fire, road accident or need for rescue.[cite: 1]

question: Under what conditions is a marriage legally recognized?
options: If it is celebrated in church or mosque
If it is celebrated by the mayor (or an elected official) at the town hall
If it is celebrated with family
explication: Only civil marriage, celebrated at the town hall by a civil registrar, is recognized by law in France.[cite: 1]

question: When should you register your child at the town hall's civil status service?
explication: Birth declaration is compulsory for every child born in France and must be done at the town hall of the place of birth within 5 days.[cite: 1]

question: Undeclared work (moonlighting) is:
explication: Concealed work is a serious offense. The worker loses all his social rights (no accident coverage, no retirement, no unemployment).[cite: 1]

question: What is the SMIC?
explication: The SMIC is the legal minimum wage in France below which no employer can pay an employee.[cite: 1]

question: What is the legal working time per week?
explication: The legal working time is set at 35 hours per week. Hours worked beyond this are overtime.[cite: 1]

question: Who is helped by France Travail (formerly Pôle emploi)?
explication: France Travail is the public service in charge of supporting job seekers and paying unemployment benefits.[cite: 1]

question: Can a woman create her own business?
options: Only if she doesn't have children
explication: Equal rights guarantee women total freedom to work, to open a bank account or to create and manage a business.[cite: 1]

question: From what age can a minor start working (excluding specific exemptions)?
explication: The legal minimum age to work is 16, the age at which compulsory schooling ends, subject to having parents' agreement.[cite: 1]

question: To which organization must one request reimbursement of their health costs?
explication: The CPAM (or Health Insurance) is the public organization that manages reimbursements for medical consultations and medicines.[cite: 1]

question: Regarding access to care, which proposition is correct?
options: Public hospitals treat everyone in an emergency
Care is free for everyone without exception
You have to pay before entering the hospital
explication: The public hospital's mission is to welcome and treat anyone whose condition requires it, including in emergency situations.[cite: 1]

question: In case of a non-urgent health problem, who should be addressed first?
explication: For an ordinary illness, you must first consult your treating doctor, who can then refer you to a specialist if necessary.[cite: 1]

question: In what situations should one go to the hospital emergencies?
options: For a serious injury, violent pain or a vital emergency
explication: Hospital emergencies are reserved for very serious or vital health problems requiring immediate care.[cite: 1]

question: What is the Vitale card used for?
options: To prove your rights to Health Insurance to be reimbursed faster
To buy medicines without a prescription
explication: The Vitale card contains the patient's administrative information. Presented to the doctor or pharmacist, it allows an automatic reimbursement of care in a few days.[cite: 1]

question: What is a health mutual (complementary health) used for?
options: To complete the reimbursement of the Health Insurance so that the care costs less
explication: Health Insurance often only reimburses 70% of care. The mutual covers the remaining amount to pay (co-payment, glasses, dental care).[cite: 1]

question: Up to what age is school (instruction) compulsory in France?
explication: Instruction is compulsory for all children, girls and boys, from the age of 3 until the age of 16 completed.[cite: 1]

question: Parental authority provides for the obligation:
options: To protect the child in his safety, his health and his morality
To register one's child in a religion
explication: Parental authority is a set of rights and duties aiming at the best interests of the child (education, maintenance, protection).[cite: 1]

question: To which schools do students go after elementary (primary) school?
explication: The classic school path is: nursery school, then elementary school, then middle school (from 6th to 3rd grade), and finally high school.[cite: 1]

question: What does the school offer for children newly arrived in France who do not speak French?
options: They are put in special classes where they learn French (UPE2A)
They must pay a private teacher
They go to the youngest class
explication: Public school welcomes all children. Specific systems (UPE2A classes) exist to teach them French intensively.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What are the official symbols of the French Republic?
options: The motto, the national anthem and the tricolor flag
The Eiffel Tower and the Louvre
explication: Article 2 of the Constitution defines the official symbols: the blue-white-red flag, the anthem (La Marseillaise) and the motto (Liberty, Equality, Fraternity).[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is equality?
options: Having the same salary as one's neighbor
Having the same rights and the same duties before the law
No longer having rich or poor people
explication: Equality means that the law is the same for everyone, without distinction of origin, race, religion or sex.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is one of the roles of associations?
options: Create social bonds and defend causes (sports, culture, solidarity)
explication: Freedom of association allows citizens to group together to share a passion or defend a cause of general interest.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Which symbol of the French Republic is tricolor?
explication: The national flag, emblem of the Republic, is composed of three colors: blue, white and red.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the date of the French national holiday?
explication: July 14 was chosen as the national holiday to commemorate the storming of the Bastille (1789) and the Fête de la Fédération (1790).[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the motto of the French Republic?
explication: Inherited from the Revolution, this motto summarizes the fundamental values that unite French citizens.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the place of the French language in the Republic?
options: It is one language among others
It is the language of the Republic, used for laws and public services
It is mandatory only at school
It is reserved for French citizens
explication: The Constitution affirms that French is the only official language of the State, laws and justice.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What freedom allows everyone to express their ideas?
explication: Freedom of expression allows one to say and write one's opinions, on condition of not defaming or calling for hatred.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Which statement is correct? Freedom of expression:
options: Allows one to say everything without limit
Is limited by law to protect others (prohibition of insults, racism)
explication: Freedom of expression stops where offenses begin: racism, antisemitism, sexism, calls for violence are punished.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What are the colors of the French flag?
explication: The colors of the national flag, set since the French Revolution, are blue, white and red.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is the Marseillaise?
explication: It is a patriotic song written in 1792 by Rouget de Lisle, which became the official national anthem of the Republic.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Who is Marianne?
options: The symbolic figure of the French Republic
explication: Marianne is an allegory (a symbol). She embodies the Republic, freedom and democracy. Her bust can be found in town halls.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: "France is an indivisible, ..., democratic and social Republic". Complete this sentence extracted from Article 1 of the Constitution:
explication: Secularism is a pillar of the Constitution. The State is neutral and guarantees the freedom of conscience of everyone.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What right is guaranteed by secularism?
options: The right to have a job
explication: Secularism allows every citizen to believe in the religion of their choice, to change it, or not to have any religion at all.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Why must the principle of secularism be respected at school?
options: To protect students from proselytism and guarantee equality
To ban all religions in France
To force students to eat the same thing
explication: Public school is neutral. Conspicuous religious symbols are forbidden there to protect students from pressure and guarantee an egalitarian learning space.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: What is secularism?
options: The separation of the State and religions
The prohibition of praying at home
The obligation to go to church
explication: Secularism is the separation of Churches and the State (1905 law). The State neither subsidizes nor privileges any cult.[cite: 1]

categorie: Principles and values of the Republic
question: Does a person have the right not to believe in a religion?
options: Yes, this is what is called atheism, protected by secularism
Only if she was born in France
explication: Freedom of conscience totally includes the right not to believe in God and to be atheist or agnostic.[cite: 1]

question: When are the senators elected?
options: They are not elected, they are appointed
explication: The Senate is elected by indirect universal suffrage. It is other elected officials of the Republic who vote to choose the senators.[cite: 1]

question: Who is elected during the presidential elections?
options: The Head of State (the President of the Republic)
explication: The presidential election allows French citizens to choose the President of the Republic by direct universal suffrage.[cite: 1]

question: For how long are the deputies elected?
explication: The deputies of the National Assembly are elected for a 5-year term during the legislative elections.[cite: 1]

question: For how long are the senators elected?
explication: The term of senators lasts 6 years. The Senate is renewed by half every three years.[cite: 1]

question: What condition is necessary to vote in elections?
options: Be registered on the electoral rolls
explication: To be able to vote, one must be an adult, have French nationality and be registered on the electoral rolls of one's commune.[cite: 1]

question: Who can vote in elections in France?
explication: The right to vote in national elections is reserved for French citizens aged at least 18 and enjoying their civil rights.[cite: 1]

question: What does "universal suffrage" mean?
options: The right to vote belongs to all citizens without distinction
Voting is compulsory under penalty of fine
explication: Universal suffrage guarantees that every citizen (man or woman, rich or poor) has an equal vote in elections.[cite: 1]

question: Regarding political parties, which proposition is correct?
options: Citizens are free to create or join the political party of their choice
The President's authorization is required to create one
explication: Political pluralism is the basis of democracy. The Constitution guarantees the freedom of formation and activity of political parties.[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of the deputies?
options: Propose, discuss and vote on laws at the National Assembly
explication: Deputies represent the Nation. They pass laws and monitor the action of the Government.[cite: 1]

question: The separation of powers is a fundamental principle. What are the three powers involved?
explication: To prevent dictatorship, the power to make laws (legislative), to execute them (executive) and to dispense justice (judicial) are separated.[cite: 1]

question: Who elects the deputies?
explication: Deputies are elected directly by French citizens by universal suffrage.[cite: 1]

question: Who votes the laws?
explication: The voting of the law belongs to the legislative power, that is to say to the Parliament which represents the citizens.[cite: 1]

question: Who resides at the Élysée Palace?
explication: The Élysée Palace is the official residence and workplace of the President of the French Republic in Paris.[cite: 1]

question: How many departments are there in France?
explication: France has 101 departments: 96 in metropolitan France and 5 overseas departments.[cite: 1]

question: Who represents the State in a department?
explication: The Prefect, appointed by the President of the Republic, represents the State and the Government in each department.[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of Parliament?
options: Vote laws and monitor government action
explication: The Parliament (composed of the National Assembly and the Senate) holds legislative power. Its members debate and vote laws.[cite: 1]

question: What is the political regime of France today?
explication: France is a democratic Republic: power belongs to the people who exercise it through their elected representatives.[cite: 1]

question: Which State is not a member of the European Union?
explication: Following the referendum on 'Brexit', the United Kingdom officially left the European Union in 2020.[cite: 1]

question: What condition is necessary to vote in the European elections?
options: Be a citizen of a European Union country and be registered on the electoral rolls
Be married to a European citizen
explication: European Union citizens residing in France can vote in European (and municipal) elections if they are registered on the electoral rolls.[cite: 1]

question: How often are European elections held?
explication: Elections to choose Members of the European Parliament take place every 5 years in all EU member countries.[cite: 1]

question: Which country is a founding country of the European Union?
explication: France, along with Germany, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands and Luxembourg, is one of the 6 founding countries of European construction.[cite: 1]

question: Who elects the Members of the European Parliament?
options: The citizens of the member countries of the European Union
The president of the European Commission
explication: Members of the European Parliament are elected by direct universal suffrage by the citizens of the 27 Member States of the European Union.[cite: 1]

question: When is Europe Day celebrated?
explication: Europe Day is celebrated on May 9, in memory of Robert Schuman's founding declaration made on May 9, 1950.[cite: 1]

question: What is the name of the current Constitution of France?
options: The Constitution of the Fifth Republic
explication: The Constitution of the Fifth Republic, adopted in 1958, is the supreme text that organizes the current French institutions.[cite: 1]

question: Regarding individual rights, which statement is correct?
options: They are guaranteed by law for all people
They are reserved for French citizens
They do not exist in France
explication: In France, fundamental rights (right to security, justice, freedom of expression) are guaranteed for all persons present on the territory.[cite: 1]

question: Which of these rights is a fundamental right?
options: The right to drive without a license
The right not to pay taxes
explication: Freedom of expression, freedom of conscience, the right to security and the right to a fair trial are fundamental rights.[cite: 1]

question: Among these texts, which one guarantees rights and freedoms in France?
explication: The Constitution is the fundamental law of the State. It guarantees the rights and freedoms of citizens and organizes political power.[cite: 1]

question: What is freedom of expression?
options: The right to publicly insult someone
The right to freely express one's ideas in compliance with the law
The right to lie before a judge
explication: Everyone has the right to express their opinions. However, the law prohibits insult, defamation or incitement to hatred and violence.[cite: 1]

question: What is the founding text establishing in France the rights and duties of every citizen?
options: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
explication: Adopted in 1789, the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (DDHC) is the major text that lays the foundations of our fundamental freedoms.[cite: 1]

question: What text was adopted during the French Revolution?
options: The Constitution of the Fifth Republic
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
The abolition of the death penalty
explication: The Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in August 1789, at the very beginning of the French Revolution.[cite: 1]

question: What freedom allows a person not to have a religion?
explication: Freedom of conscience guarantees the right to choose one's religion, to change it, or to be atheist (not believing in any god).[cite: 1]

question: Is it always possible to get a divorce?
options: Yes, the law allows divorce for all married couples
Only if the marriage lasted 10 years
explication: The right to divorce is guaranteed by French law, whether by mutual consent or following a judge's decision.[cite: 1]

question: Regarding the limits to individual freedoms, which statement is correct?
options: They can be limited by law to protect public order and the rights of others
Only the mayor can limit them
explication: One person's freedom ends where another's begins. The law sets limits to guarantee the safety and respect of everyone.[cite: 1]

question: Should you reduce your waste?
options: Yes, it is an ecological responsibility to protect the environment
No, the state takes care of it
explication: Reducing, sorting and recycling your waste is a major civic duty to preserve the planet and limit pollution.[cite: 1]

question: Why can individual freedoms be limited?
options: To protect the general interest, public security and the rights of others
explication: The State can restrict certain freedoms (such as freedom of movement during a pandemic) to protect the safety or health of all.[cite: 1]

question: What must a person do in case of an accident?
options: Protect the scene of the accident and immediately alert the emergency services
explication: Assistance to a person in danger is a legal obligation. You must secure the area and call 15 (SAMU), 18 (Firefighters) or 112.[cite: 1]

question: What does French citizenship allow?
options: The right to vote and to be elected in national elections
The right not to respect the laws
The right to work without a contract
explication: Being a French citizen gives fundamental political rights, including the right to vote in all elections and the right to stand as a candidate.[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of the police?
options: Vote the laws at the Assembly
Maintain public order, protect the population and search for perpetrators of offenses
explication: The police and the gendarmerie ensure the safety of citizens. It is then the judges who decide on the criminal convictions.[cite: 1]

question: What is an offense?
options: An act forbidden and punished by law
explication: An offense is the violation of a law. It is classified into three categories according to its severity: contravention, misdemeanor and crime.[cite: 1]

question: How can you reduce your waste?
options: By burning them in the street
By sorting waste, recycling and avoiding waste
By giving them to your neighbors
explication: Selective sorting (separating glass, cardboard, plastic) and recycling are compulsory and essential for the environment.[cite: 1]

question: Leaving a broken washing machine on the sidewalk is:
options: An offense (illegal dumping) punishable by a heavy fine
explication: The abandonment of bulky waste on the public highway is prohibited. They must be deposited at the recycling center or use the town hall's 'bulky items' service.[cite: 1]

question: What does human trafficking consist of?
options: Recruiting or exploiting people by force or threat (forced labor, sexual exploitation)
explication: Human trafficking (modern slavery, prostitution networks) is an extremely serious crime, severely punished by the Penal Code.[cite: 1]

question: What is the most serious offense?
explication: Criminal law classifies offenses into three levels: contravention (the least serious), misdemeanor (more serious), and crime (the most serious, judged by the Assize Court).[cite: 1]

question: After getting a driver's license, what must you do to be able to drive your car?
options: Ask the town hall for permission
Pay a tax to the police
explication: It is strictly forbidden to drive without insurance. Civil liability insurance is mandatory to compensate victims in the event of an accident.[cite: 1]

question: What must an employer do to set a salary?
options: He can pay what he wants
He must compulsorily respect the legal minimum wage (the SMIC)
He sets the salary according to nationality
explication: The law protects workers. No full-time salary can be lower than the SMIC (Salaire Minimum Interprofessionnel de Croissance).[cite: 1]

question: What is the first step to take to look for a job?
options: Register with France Travail (formerly Pôle emploi)
explication: France Travail is the public employment service. Registration allows you to be supported in your research and, under conditions, to receive benefits.[cite: 1]

question: Can a foreign person in a regular situation create their own business?
options: Yes, if their residence permit authorizes them to carry out a professional or commercial activity
Only if they are married to a French person
Yes, but without the right to hire
explication: Business creation is possible for foreigners, provided they hold a suitable residence permit (e.g. resident card, 'entrepreneur' mention, etc.).[cite: 1]

question: What is an emergency number?
options: A free phone number, reachable 24 hours a day, to alert the emergency services (15, 17, 18, 112)
A number to find a doctor on Sunday
explication: Emergency numbers allow you to immediately contact the police, the SAMU or the firefighters. They are free and work even without credit.[cite: 1]

question: What is the role of the treating doctor?
options: Provide regular medical follow-up and refer to specialists if necessary
Check sick leave for the boss
explication: The treating doctor (often a general practitioner) is the pillar of the care path in France. They know you, treat you on a daily basis and refer you to specialists.[cite: 1]

question: What is the purpose of mandatory vaccinations?
options: Protect the child against serious diseases and prevent epidemics in the population
It's just a formality for traveling
explication: In France, 11 vaccines are mandatory for children. They protect public health by eradicating fatal diseases (tetanus, measles, polio...).[cite: 1]

question: For whom is school compulsory?
options: For children who already speak French
For all children, girls and boys, aged 3 to 16
For children of French nationality only
explication: The right to education is universal in France. Instruction is compulsory from 3 to 16 years old, regardless of nationality or the situation of the parents.[cite: 1]

question: What diploma is generally obtained at the end of high school?
explication: The Baccalaureate marks the end of secondary studies in high school and opens the doors to higher education (universities, grandes écoles).[cite: 1]

question: A child enrolled in school:
options: Is subject to a requirement of attendance (they must be present every day of class)
Can miss classes if they are tired
explication: School attendance is mandatory. Absences must always be justified by the parents (e.g. medical certificate).[cite: 1]

question: Which writer is French?
explication: Victor Hugo is one of the most illustrious French writers, author of masterpieces like 'Les Misérables' or 'Notre-Dame de Paris'.[cite: 1]

question: What power does a judge hold?
options: The power to direct the police
explication: The judge exercises judicial power. They are independent, interpret the law and settle conflicts or sanction offenses.[cite: 1]

question: The judicial authority is exercised by:
explication: The judicial institution is composed of different jurisdictions (judicial court, court of appeal, assize court) animated by independent magistrates.[cite: 1]

question: Who holds executive power?
options: The President of the Republic and the Government
explication: Executive power is responsible for conducting the policy of the Nation and executing the laws. It is directed by the President and the Prime Minister.[cite: 1]

question: Who founded the Fifth Republic?
explication: General de Gaulle is the founder of the Fifth Republic in 1958, which provided France with a new Constitution and strong executive power.[cite: 1]

question: Which war took place between 1914 and 1918?
explication: The First World War (or Great War) took place from 1914 to 1918, mainly opposing the Allies to the Central Powers.[cite: 1]

question: What is Paris?
options: The capital of France and the seat of national institutions
explication: Paris is the political capital (Elysée, Parliament) and the largest city in France.[cite: 1]

question: Children who do not speak French:
options: Are sent back to their country
Are taken care of by the public school in specialized classes to learn the language (UPE2A)
explication: The school of the Republic is inclusive. Pedagogical Units for Incoming Allophone Pupils (UPE2A) exist to teach them French.[cite: 1]

question: Which city is located by the Mediterranean Sea?
explication: Nice, like Marseille and Toulon, is a large city on the southern coast of France, bordered by the Mediterranean.[cite: 1]

question: Who has the power to sanction the perpetrator of a theft?
options: A magistrate (a judge) in a court
explication: In France, the police arrest suspects, but only a judge has the power to pronounce a sentence or a criminal sanction at the end of a trial.[cite: 1]

question: Equality between men and women in France is:
options: Guaranteed by the Constitution and the law
explication: The principle of strict equality between women and men is enshrined in the Preamble to the 1946 Constitution and applies to all areas of life.[cite: 1]

question: The right to strike allows:
options: To stop work collectively to defend professional demands
explication: The right to strike is a constitutional right in France. It allows employees to peacefully put pressure to improve their working conditions.[cite: 1]

question: In case of a dispute (conflict) with your employer regarding your salary, which court should you go to?
explication: The industrial tribunal (Conseil des Prud'hommes) is the specialized jurisdiction in France to settle individual conflicts related to the employment contract between employers and employees.[cite: 1]
